prompt:
Develop a Meridian Operations Hub system for a mid-sized US direct-to-consumer retailer that must run entirely on an isolated local network while still supporting hiring, fulfillment, and customer support end to end. Users include System Administrators, HR Recruiters, Hiring Managers, Warehouse Clerks, Customer Service Agents, and Compliance Officers, each seeing only the modules permitted by their role. The H5 mobile web experience is the primary interface for floor and warehouse staff and is rendered with Templ templates in a responsive layout that adapts cleanly from phones to desktops, using a unified component library and theme tokens for consistent colors, typography, and error states. Recruiters can create job postings and capture applications from multiple local entry points (manual entry, QR code kiosk form on the intranet, or CSV import), then move candidates through a configurable multi-stage pipeline (for example Screening, Invitation, Test, Interview, Offer, Hire, Reject, Onboarding) with employer-defined stages, required fields, and screening rules; blocklists can automatically flag or stop progression based on email domain, duplicate identity matches, or keywords configured by HR. Customer Service Agents open after-sales tickets tied to an order, selecting return-and-refund or refund-only, attaching proof (up to 5 files, JPG/PNG/PDF, 10 MB each) and seeing SLA timers and priority; the UI supports offline drafts in local cache, optimistic status updates with rollback on failure, and weak-network retries with user-visible conflict prompts. Warehouse Clerks manage multi-warehouse and sub-warehouse inventory through inbound, outbound, transfers, and cycle counts, with clear on-screen confirmation and low-stock warnings when on-hand falls below the safety stock threshold (default 20 units or a role-configured value).

The backend uses Gin to expose decoupled, REST-style APIs consumed by the H5 pages, with PostgreSQL as the local system of record for candidates, pipeline events, tickets, inventory balances, reservations, and an immutable inventory ledger. Orders reserve stock immediately and create a reservation hold that auto-releases on cancellation or after 2 hours without confirmation; transfers and counts require a reason code and produce auditable ledger entries that cannot be edited, only reversed. After-sales decisions are rule-driven and deterministic: returns are eligible within 30 days of delivery timestamp, refund-only is enforced for non-returnable item categories, and tickets auto-escalate if not assigned within 4 business hours for High priority or 1 business day for Standard. Security is offline-first: login is local username/password only (minimum 12 characters, lockout after 5 failed attempts for 15 minutes), sessions use JWT with refresh tokens, and sensitive actions (role/permission changes, export, delete, refund approval) require step-up verification by re-entering the password within 5 minutes. All API requests include request signing, anti-replay nonces with a 2-minute acceptance window, and idempotency keys for order creation and refunds to prevent duplicates. Compliance controls include a local "crawler" that only indexes approved folders for attachments and resumes, capped at 5,000 files per nightly run, honoring opt-out markers and frequency limits; sensitive fields such as SSN are detected by pattern and masked to last four digits in the UI and logs. Data retention defaults to 2 years for rejected candidates and 7 years for financial/support records, with deletion requests processed within 30 days, and all sensitive data is encrypted at rest with locally managed keys and full audit trails retained for compliance review.
